startScreenOptions.title: PY3.1-PY3.9 — Interactive Video (PY)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive video with 6 embedded questions for PY3.1-PY3.9.

interactions.label: Which factor is the PRIMARY reason the resting membrane potential sits close to

params.question: Which factor is the PRIMARY reason the resting membrane potential sits close to −70 mV rather than 0 mV?

answers.text: The membrane is far more permeable to potassium than to sodium at rest, allowing K⁺ to diffuse out

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. At rest, open K⁺ leak channels dominate membrane permeability, so K⁺ exits down its gradient and leaves the interior negative, approaching the K⁺ Nernst potential.

answers.text: The Na⁺/K⁺ pump directly generates the entire resting potential by pumping ions electrogenically

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The pump contributes only a small electrogenic component (3 Na⁺ out for 2 K⁺ in). Its main role is maintaining the gradients that K⁺ leak channels exploit.

answers.text: Voltage-gated sodium channels remain slightly open, letting Na⁺ leak inward

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels are closed at rest. A small background Na⁺ leak exists but only slightly offsets the dominant K⁺ effect.

answers.text: Chloride ions are actively pumped out of the cell, leaving the interior negative

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Cl⁻ distribution is largely passive in most neurons and is not the principal determinant of the resting potential.

overallFeedback.feedback: Remember: at rest, the membrane is most permeable to K⁺. The resting potential therefore sits near E_K (−94 mV), offset slightly by Na⁺ leak to about −70 mV.
The resting potential is essentially a potassium diffusion potential, modified slightly by Na⁺ leak and the electrogenic pump.

metadata.title: Which factor is the PRIMARY reason the r

interactions.label: In multiple sclerosis, nerve conduction velocity is markedly slowed. What is the

params.question: In multiple sclerosis, nerve conduction velocity is markedly slowed. What is the underlying mechanism?

answers.text: Axonal transection prevents any electrical signal from propagating

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: In MS the axons are relatively preserved; it is the myelin sheath that is damaged. Complete transection would abolish conduction, not merely slow it.

answers.text: Loss of myelin disrupts saltatory conduction, forcing the impulse back to slow continuous conduction

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Without intact myelin, the action potential cannot jump between nodes of Ranvier, so conduction reverts to slow point-to-point propagation.

answers.text: Voltage-gated sodium channels are destroyed at the axon hillock

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: MS does not selectively destroy axon hillock Na⁺ channels. The primary lesion is demyelination of internodes.

answers.text: The sodium–potassium pump fails, collapsing ion gradients

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The Na⁺/K⁺ pump is not the target in MS. Gradients remain intact; it is saltatory conduction that is disrupted.

overallFeedback.feedback: Recall: myelin acts as an insulator so that current flows only at nodes of Ranvier. Strip the myelin and saltatory conduction fails — conduction slows dramatically even though the axon is intact.
Demyelination slows conduction by abolishing the node-to-node jumping of action potentials characteristic of myelinated fibres.

interactions.label: At the neuromuscular junction, what event immediately triggers acetylcholine rel

params.question: At the neuromuscular junction, what event immediately triggers acetylcholine release from the axon terminal?

answers.text: Binding of acetylcholine to nicotinic receptors on the motor end plate

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This occurs AFTER release, on the postsynaptic side. It generates the end-plate potential, not the release itself.

answers.text: Opening of voltage-gated potassium channels in the axon terminal

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: K⁺ channel opening repolarises the terminal; it does not trigger vesicle fusion.

answers.text: Influx of calcium through voltage-gated calcium channels in the presynaptic terminal

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Depolarisation of the terminal opens voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels; the inrush of Ca²⁺ drives SNARE-mediated vesicle fusion and ACh release.

answers.text: Activation of acetylcholinesterase in the synaptic cleft

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Acetylcholinesterase terminates the signal by hydrolysing ACh after release — it plays no role in triggering release.

overallFeedback.feedback: Remember the sequence: action potential → terminal depolarisation → voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels open → Ca²⁺ influx → vesicle fusion → ACh release.
Calcium influx is the key trigger linking terminal depolarisation to neurotransmitter release at every chemical synapse, including the NMJ.

metadata.title: At the neuromuscular junction, what even

interactions.label: In the cross-bridge cycle of skeletal muscle, ATP binding to the myosin head cau

params.question: In the cross-bridge cycle of skeletal muscle, ATP binding to the myosin head causes it to DETACH from actin, not to perform the power stroke.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Review the cycle: the power stroke occurs when Pi is released from an already-attached myosin head. ATP is needed AFTER the stroke, to detach the head. In rigor mortis, absence of ATP locks myosin on actin.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. ATP binding detaches myosin from actin; hydrolysis then re-cocks the head. The power stroke itself is driven by release of inorganic phosphate, not ATP binding. This is why rigor mortis occurs — without ATP, myosin cannot detach.

metadata.title: In the cross-bridge cycle of skeletal mu

interactions.label: According to Henneman's size principle, how does the nervous system initially pr

params.question: According to Henneman's size principle, how does the nervous system initially produce finely graded, low-force contractions?

answers.text: By recruiting large motor units first, then adding smaller ones as force demand rises

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This reverses the size principle. Large units would produce coarse, powerful contractions — incompatible with fine control.

answers.text: By recruiting small motor units first, adding progressively larger ones as more force is required

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Small, low-threshold motor units are recruited first to enable fine control; larger units are added later to produce greater force.

answers.text: By increasing the firing rate of a single motor unit without recruiting any additional units

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Rate coding contributes to force gradation, but the size principle specifically describes the ORDER of motor unit recruitment, not rate changes alone.

answers.text: By simultaneously activating all motor units at submaximal intensity

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Simultaneous activation would produce an all-or-none response at the whole-muscle level and prevent fine grading of force.

overallFeedback.feedback: The size principle: small motor units (low threshold) are recruited first for fine control, then progressively larger ones. Rate coding (wave summation, tetanus) is a SECOND mechanism of force gradation.
Small-first recruitment enables smooth, precise control for delicate movements; larger units join only as force demand grows.

interactions.label: An athlete sprints maximally for 8 seconds. Which energy system is the PRIMARY s

params.question: An athlete sprints maximally for 8 seconds. Which energy system is the PRIMARY source of ATP during this burst?

answers.text: Aerobic oxidative phosphorylation in the mitochondria

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Oxidative phosphorylation is the dominant source only for sustained efforts beyond 2 minutes. It is too slow to meet the demands of an 8-second sprint.

answers.text: Anaerobic glycolysis generating lactate

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Anaerobic glycolysis becomes the main source roughly between 10 seconds and 2 minutes. It is not the primary supplier during the first 8 seconds.

answers.text: The phosphocreatine system via creatine kinase

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. For the first 8–10 seconds, phosphocreatine rapidly regenerates ATP through creatine kinase — the fastest available source, ideal for short explosive bursts.

answers.text: Beta-oxidation of fatty acids

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Fatty acid oxidation is slow and suits prolonged, low-to-moderate intensity exercise — not the first seconds of a sprint.

overallFeedback.feedback: Remember the timeline: phosphocreatine (0–10 s) → anaerobic glycolysis (10 s–2 min) → aerobic oxidative phosphorylation (>2 min).
The phosphocreatine system dominates the first 8–10 seconds of all-out effort; glycolysis and oxidative phosphorylation take over progressively thereafter.
